behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: The failure boundary includes real `git` rename detection output plus CLI parsing/categorization, so the behavior spans subprocess + repository state, not just one local function. Rationale B: A mocked diff payload can hide real rename formats (e.g., similarity scores/path encoding), so MOCK-HIDE applies. REAL-INFRA is required because behavior text is about repository changes as observed by the CLI. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI/ML decision appears in the behavior text; Integration/Unit are rejected because the wording depends on true git-produced rename semantics, not only in-process handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp real git repo, commit a file, rename it with `git mv`, run the CLI, and assert `renamed=1` while `added`/`deleted` do not include that file.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: Crash risk is typically at real subprocess/output-decoding boundaries when git emits binary diff metadata, so REAL-INFRA is the minimum reliable boundary. Rationale B: Stubbing binary diff text can miss encoding/format quirks that only real git output triggers; MOCK-HIDE applies. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified by behavior text (no model output). Integration/Unit rejected because crash condition is tied to actual git process output on binary content.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, commit baseline, add/modify a true binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI, verify process exits successfully and binary file is included in counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: This behavior depends on unborn-HEAD git behavior and exit/status handling from real commands, which is a subprocess/system boundary. Rationale B: Mocking command responses can miss the exact no-commit failure mode and stderr/exit-code interactions; MOCK-HIDE applies. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI). Integration/Unit rejected because behavior text explicitly scopes a real repository lifecycle state (no commits yet).
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repo with zero commits, run the CLI, assert successful exit and an empty structured report instead of an exception/error output.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: This is end-to-end correctness against actual repository history, requiring real git history traversal and CLI execution. Rationale B: In-process fixtures can validate counting logic but can still miss discrepancies introduced by real git command selection/output interpretation; MOCK-HIDE applies. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified because no model-based judgment is involved. Integration/Unit rejected because behavior text anchors truth to the real repository change history, not synthetic objects.
behaviors.test_description: Build a temp repo with known sequence of commits containing add/delete/rename/modify events, run the CLI for the same range, and assert exact category counts match expected history-derived totals.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: Correctness depends on real git index vs working-tree state, which is an external tool/state boundary requiring actual staging operations. Rationale B: Mocking staged/unstaged snapshots can hide bugs in how the CLI invokes git for each mode; MOCK-HIDE applies. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI). Integration/Unit rejected because the behavior text explicitly compares two real repository states managed by git internals.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, create identical content changes in two runs (one staged, one unstaged), execute the CLI in each state, and assert the full structured stdout payloads are equal.
